reason: sqrt(log n) grows strictly faster than log log n, so it is NOT O(log log n); A, C, D are all true statements.
Height of a tree = number of edges on the longest path from root to deepest leaf; A is level, B is height of a node, D is depth.
OSPF is a link-state protocol and runs Dijkstra's SPF; RIP/IGRP/BGP are distance/path-vector.

The operative difference is that graphs may contain cycles/loops, forcing a visited-set; 'trees have root' is true but is not why traversal algorithms differ. Note the source's own explanation ('As trees do not have loops, they are easier to traverse') argues for B while its key says C.
BFS uses a FIFO queue; DFS uses a stack.
With root at height 0, levels 0..k hold 2^0+...+2^k = 2^(k+1)-1 nodes. (Options A and D are literal duplicates, a flaw, but B is unambiguously correct.)
A queue is First-In-First-Out.
Bubble-sort swap count = inversion count of {2,5,1,3,4}: (2,1),(5,1),(5,3),(5,4) = 4.
A doubly linked node stores two separate pointers (next and prev); one pointer cannot hold two addresses.
A spanning tree keeps n-1 edges, so at most e-(n-1) = e-n+1 edges can be removed.
Insertion sort is O(n) on already-sorted input; merge/heap stay O(n log n) and naive quicksort degrades to O(n^2).
Standard quicksort partitioning is not stable; bubble, merge and insertion sort are stable.
Hash table gives average-case O(1) lookup; tree/heap/linked-list do not.
Queue, stack and array are all linear data structures.
DFS visits every vertex and scans every adjacency entry: Theta(|V|+|E|).
Linked-list deletion (given node), hash-table search and adjacency-matrix edge insertion are all O(1); heapify/build-heap is not constant time.
Shell sort is gapped insertion sort and reduces to plain insertion sort at gap 1.
Every spanning tree of G spans all n vertices and has exactly n-1 edges, independent of algorithm.
Any recursive algorithm can be rewritten iteratively with an explicit stack, so none of the listed algorithms requires recursion.

Flawed question: the true worst case is O(log n), which is not offered. All four options are technically valid upper bounds, so the tightest available is O(n) = D. The source's explanation ('compares all n values') is wrong reasoning even though D is the least-bad option.
